name: Smith Mine (Bear Creek)
article: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
description: Site of the Smith Mine disaster, February 27, 1943. An explosion killed 74 miners. The mine served the Montana, Wyoming and Southern Railroad coal branch.

name: Red Lodge Depot (Montana, Wyoming and Southern RR)
article: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
description: Southern terminus of the coal branch line from Billings. Red Lodge was the supply and service town for the Bear Creek coal mines.

name: Custer Creek Train Wreck Site
article: The Custer Creek Train Wreck of 1938
description: Site of the June 19, 1938 wreck of the Milwaukee Road's Olympian passenger train. Flash flooding washed out a bridge over Custer Creek. 47 passengers killed. Near Saugus, Prairie County.

name: Northern Pacific Completion Site, Gold Creek
article: The Golden Spike at Gold Creek
description: Site of the Northern Pacific Railroad completion ceremony, September 8, 1883. Henry Villard drove the final spike in the presence of Ulysses S. Grant and 300 guests. Now a National Historic Landmark.

name: Butte — BA&P Eastern Terminus
article: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
description: Eastern terminus of the Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway at the Butte mine yards. The BA&P hauled copper ore from the mines to the Anaconda smelter 26 miles west. Electrified 1913.

name: Anaconda Copper Smelter
article: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
description: The Anaconda Copper smelter, western terminus of the BA&P. Marcus Daly built the smelter in 1884 to process Butte copper ore. The 585-foot Anaconda Stack still stands as a state park.

name: Harlowton — Jawbone Railroad Western Terminus
article: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
description: Western terminus of the Montana Railroad (the 'Jawbone'), built by Richard Harlow. The line ran from Lombard on the Northern Pacific to Lewistown. Harlow financed it largely on talk — hence the nickname.

name: Lewistown — Jawbone Railroad Eastern Terminus
article: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
description: Eastern terminus of the Jawbone Railroad at Lewistown, the largest city in central Montana. The line served the agricultural and mining communities of the Judith Basin.

name: Marias Pass Summit
article: The Discovery of Marias Pass
description: Marias Pass, elevation 5,213 ft — the lowest crossing of the Continental Divide in the northern Rocky Mountains. John Frank Stevens discovered it for the Great Northern Railway on December 11, 1889, walking alone in temperatures of -40°F.

name: John Frank Stevens Monument
article: The Discovery of Marias Pass
description: Monument to John Frank Stevens at Marias Pass, honoring his solo winter discovery of the pass in 1889. A statue of Theodore Roosevelt also stands nearby.

name: Harlowton — Eastern End of Rocky Mountain Electrification
article: The Milwaukee Road Electrification
description: Harlowton was the eastern end of the Milwaukee Road's Rocky Mountain Division electrification (1915). Electric locomotives took over here from steam for the mountain crossing to Avery, Idaho.

name: Billings — Northern Pacific Land Office
article: Sodbusters and the Railroad Land Promoters
description: Billings was platted by the Northern Pacific in 1882 and named for railroad president Frederick Billings. The NP's land office here sold railroad grant lands to homesteaders lured by promotional literature promising adequate rainfall and fertile soil.

name: Livingston — Northern Pacific Division Point
article: Sodbusters and the Railroad Land Promoters
description: Livingston was the Northern Pacific's division point for the Yellowstone branch and the gateway to Yellowstone National Park. The 1902 depot still stands. Named for NP director Crawford Livingston.

name: Helena — Chinese Railroad Worker Community
article: The Chinese Forerunners of the Northern Pacific
description: Helena's Chinatown was established in part by Chinese workers who graded the Northern Pacific right-of-way through the mountains. An estimated 15,000 Chinese laborers worked on the NP's Montana construction in the 1880s.

name: Missoula — Northern Pacific Mountain Division
article: The Chinese Forerunners of the Northern Pacific
description: Missoula was a key point on the Northern Pacific's mountain division, where Chinese laborers performed much of the grading through the Clark Fork canyon. The Missoula NP depot (1901) still stands.

name: Browning — Blackfeet Reservation (Great Northern Route)
article: The Iron Horse and the Blackfeet Nation
description: Browning is the seat of the Blackfeet Nation. The Great Northern Railway crossed Blackfeet territory under an 1895 agreement that ceded the mineral strip along the Rocky Mountain Front — land that became the eastern portion of Glacier National Park in 1910.

name: Cut Bank — Great Northern / Blackfeet Territory
article: The Iron Horse and the Blackfeet Nation
description: Cut Bank sits on the edge of the Blackfeet Reservation. The Great Northern's Hi-Line route passed through here, bisecting Blackfeet hunting territory and accelerating the end of the buffalo economy.

name: East Glacier Park Station
article: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
description: East Glacier Park Station, built 1912. Gateway to Glacier Park Lodge, the Great Northern's flagship hotel. Still an active Amtrak Empire Builder stop (seasonal). The station and lodge were the centerpiece of the GN's 'See America First' tourism campaign.

name: Whitefish Station (Empire Builder)
article: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
description: Whitefish Station, the western gateway to Glacier National Park on the Great Northern/BNSF main line. Active Amtrak Empire Builder stop. The 1927 depot is on the National Register of Historic Places.

name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — East Portal (Montana)
article: The St. Paul Pass Tunnel
description: East portal of the St. Paul Pass (Taft) Tunnel on the Montana-Idaho border. 8,771 feet long, completed 1908. The Milwaukee Road bored through the Bitterroot Range at 4,157 ft elevation. Now the eastern trailhead of the Route of the Hiawatha bike trail.

name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — West Portal (Idaho)
article: The St. Paul Pass Tunnel
description: West portal of the St. Paul Pass Tunnel near Avery, Idaho. The tunnel was the key engineering achievement of the Milwaukee Road's Pacific Extension. Now the western end of the Route of the Hiawatha trail.

name: Harlowton — Milwaukee Road Abandonment Point
article: The Longest Ghost: Abandoning the Milwaukee Road
description: Harlowton was the eastern end of the Milwaukee Road's Pacific Extension in Montana. When the railroad abandoned its western lines in 1980, the tracks were pulled up and the right-of-way fell silent. The Milwaukee Road depot still stands.

name: Fort Benton — Missouri River Levee
article: The End of the Steamboat Era at Fort Benton
description: Fort Benton's levee on the Missouri River — the farthest inland port in North America. Steamboats traveled 2,600 miles from St. Louis to reach here. The arrival of the Great Northern Railway in 1887 ended the steamboat era. The levee and historic district are preserved.

name: Fort Benton — Great Northern Depot
article: The End of the Steamboat Era at Fort Benton
description: The Great Northern Railway reached Fort Benton in 1887, ending the steamboat trade that had sustained the town since 1846. The depot replaced the levee as the center of commerce.

name: Whitefish — Empire Builder Station
article: Forty-Five Hours Through the Mountains: The Empire Builder
description: Whitefish is the primary Montana stop for Amtrak's Empire Builder, the direct descendant of the Great Northern's flagship train inaugurated June 10, 1929. The train runs Chicago to Seattle/Portland, 2,206 miles.

name: Butte — Miners Union Hall Site (1914 Riot)
article: The Richest Hill on Earth: Butte, 1914
description: Site of the Butte Miners Union Hall, dynamited June 13, 1914. The WFM Local No. 1 had stood for 30 years. Its destruction ended union recognition at the Anaconda mines and began six years of National Guard occupation. The site is now a parking lot.

name: Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine Memorial
article: The Richest Hill on Earth: Butte, 1914
description: Site of the Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine fire, June 8, 1917. 168 miners killed — the worst hard rock mining disaster in US history. The memorial stands on the hill above Uptown Butte.

name: Billings — NP-Platted Town, 1882
article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
description: Billings was platted by the Northern Pacific in 1882 and named for railroad president Frederick Billings. It grew to become Montana's largest city. The original NP townsite grid is still visible in the downtown street layout.

name: Miles City — Northern Pacific Division Point
article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
description: Miles City grew around the Northern Pacific's arrival in 1881. Named for General Nelson Miles, it became the cattle-shipping capital of eastern Montana and a key NP division point.

name: Glendive — Northern Pacific Division Point
article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
description: Glendive was established by the Northern Pacific in 1881 as a division point near the Montana-North Dakota border. The NP depot was the economic center of Dawson County for decades.

name: Butte — Copper Kings Mine District
article: The War of the Copper Kings
description: The Butte mine district where Marcus Daly, William A. Clark, and F. Augustus Heinze fought for control of the world's richest copper deposits from the 1880s through 1906. The Anaconda Company emerged as the monopoly. The Berkeley Pit is the legacy.

name: Anaconda — Daly's Smelter and Company Town
article: The War of the Copper Kings
description: Anaconda was Marcus Daly's company town, built around his copper smelter in 1884. The 585-foot Anaconda Stack is the tallest surviving masonry structure in the world. The BA&P Railroad connected it to Butte.

name: Great Falls — Boston and Montana Smelter
article: The War of the Copper Kings
description: Great Falls was the site of the Boston and Montana Company's smelter, the second-largest copper operation in Montana after Daly's Anaconda. The Great Northern Railway served it. The company was eventually absorbed by Anaconda.
